reasoning: The FRED analysis provides a short-term probability of 0.54 for exceeding the 4.88% threshold by April 19, driven by recent short-term upward momentum but tempered by historical seasonal effects. Additionally, recent news suggests a bearish Treasuries market due to massive debt issuance and short-end yields spiking, pointing to upward pressure on long yields over the next few months as the yield curve uninverts. Longer-term, interest rates have a strong tendency to mean-revert, and CBO long-term projections expect the 10-year Treasury to rise but average only around 4.3%-4.4%, implying that the 20-year around 4.88% is already high relative to historical long-term averages post-GFC, though term premiums and debt limits might sustain higher numbers. Following the rule for decay over time, probabilities are set to initially reflect the short-term upward momentum before smoothly regressing to 0.5 over the multi-year horizons, representing maximum uncertainty roughly evenly balanced between mean reversion and structurally higher long-term deficits.
